messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: Causal Inference with LLMs in R/Python
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: Causal Inference with LLMs in R/Python
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - This seminar offers practical methods for integrating advanced computational tools into your quantitative research workflows. Causal Inference with LLMs is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 8 and 10 with Moses Boudourides (Data Science Graduate Program, School of Professional Studies, Northwestern University). This seminar provides a principled framework for integrating large language models (LLMs) into causal inference workflows, offering new avenues for analyzing complex ecological and environmental datasets. Ecologists can apply these methods to measure latent constructs from textual field notes, generate instrumental variables from policy documents, or support causal discovery in complex environmental systems using the provided R and Python scripts. Participants will learn to construct defensible causal claims from LLM-derived variables, ensuring methodological rigor in their own research projects.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/causal-inference-with-llms) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online Course: Handling Missing Data in R (22–24 April)
messages.subject_clean: Online Course: Handling Missing Data in R (22–24 April)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
There are only a few seats left for the online Physalia course ?Handling Missing Data in R?, taking place from 22?24 April.
 
 
Missing data are a common challenge in real-world datasets and, if not handled properly, can lead to biased results and reduced statistical power. This course provides a practical and principled framework to diagnose and address missing data using R, combining solid theoretical foundations with hands-on implementation.
 
Over three interactive sessions, participants will explore:
The mechanisms behind missing data (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and their implications
Tools to diagnose and visualize missing data patterns
Traditional approaches such as deletion and simple imputation
Modern techniques including regression imputation and multiple imputation (MICE)
Advanced methods such as Soft Impute and the Expectation?Maximization (EM) algorithm
By the end of the course, participants will be able to confidently choose and apply appropriate strategies for handling missing data in their own analyses.
 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ) 
 
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Computational Genomics for Microbial Traits
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Computational Genomics for Microbial Traits
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Discover general-purpose computational approaches that can enhance your research on microbial systems and ecological data. Computational Genomics for Microbial Traits (Free Seminar) is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 22, led by Janani Ravi (University of Colorado Anschutz). This seminar introduces general-purpose computational approaches that integrate large-scale, heterogeneous public datasets to bridge the microbial genotype-phenotype gap. Researchers in ecology and environmental science can apply these methods, leveraging machine learning and comparative genomics, to analyze microbial community dynamics in diverse ecosystems, from soils to aquatic environments. Understanding microbial adaptation and host responses through these tools can advance studies on ecosystem health, pathogen spread, and environmental resilience, offering new perspectives for data analysis in R.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/computational-genomics-for-microbial-tra) to reserve your place, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last seat available – Habitat Selection from Telemetry Data (20–23 April)
messages.subject_clean: Last seat available – Habitat Selection from Telemetry Data (20–23 April)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We have just one seat left for our online course:
Analyzing Habitat Selection of Animals from Telemetry Data

Dates: 20?23 April | 14:00?18:00 (Berlin time)
 
This advanced course focuses on modeling habitat selection in R using telemetry data, including Habitat Selection Functions (HSFs) / Resource Selection Functions (RSFs) and Step Selection Functions (SSFs), along with model extensions and validation.
 
For more information: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/telemetry/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/telemetry/ ) 
 
Best regards and Happy Easter everyone,
Carlo

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Dimensionality Reduction with UMAP in R
messages.subject_clean: Dimensionality Reduction with UMAP in R
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Explore powerful techniques for simplifying complex datasets and extracting meaningful insights from your research. Dimensionality Reduction with UMAP and Beyond 2.0 led by Nikolay Oskolkov (Lund University) is now available as an on-demand streaming seminar. High-dimensional ecological and environmental datasets often present significant analytical challenges, making insights difficult to extract. This seminar provides hands-on training in dimensionality reduction techniques like UMAP, PCA, and tSNE, with practical implementation in R and Python. Ecologists and environmental scientists can apply these methods to simplify complex data, uncover hidden patterns, and enhance the interpretability and efficiency of their research.

Access this seminar (https://instats.org/seminar/dimensionality-reduction-with-umap-and-b-1) online at your convenience and we'd appreciate you sharing this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Functional Trait Space Analyses in R | 14–16 September (online)
messages.subject_clean: Functional Trait Space Analyses in R | 14–16 September (online)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are pleased to announce our upcoming online course: Functional Trait Space Analyses in R

Dates: 14?16 September 


This course provides an introduction to the theory and practice of functional trait space analyses, a key approach in trait-based ecology to explore organismal diversity and species adaptations. Participants will combine lectures with hands-on sessions in R.
 
Participants will learn to:

? Understand and apply trait space methods
? Build and interpret functional trait spaces
? Link traits to ecological patterns
? Visualize results in R
 
Sessions run daily from 14:00 to 18:00/19:00 (Berlin time). All materials will be provided.
 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/functional-trait-space-analyses-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/functional-trait-space-analyses-in-r/ )
 
Best regards,
Carlo

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Learn how to write and use functions in R
messages.subject_clean: Learn how to write and use functions in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

Transmitting Science is offering a new edition of the live online course 
"Functional programming with R".

Course webpage: 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/statistics-and-bioinformatics/functional-programming-with-r/

The increasing complexity of data management and analyses conducted in 
ecology creates the need for optimal efficiency. Functional programming 
optimises these processes for R. Functions allow the automation of 
common tasks (such as reading different databases) in a more powerful 
and general way, simplifying the code.

This course will teach participants how to write functions and employ 
them in iterative programming, using both base R and the {purrr} package 
from {tidyverse}. Additionally, participants will learn a coding style 
that facilitates understanding for their future selves and their 
collaborators while minimising errors.

For any questions, please write to courses at transmittingscience.com

Best regards,

Haris
-- 
Haris Saslis, PhD
Course Coordinator
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com [1]



Links:
------
[1] http://www.transmittingscience.com

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: RNA-seq: Counts to Conclusions
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: RNA-seq: Counts to Conclusions
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - This seminar offers practical skills for analyzing complex biological data using modern techniques. RNA-seq: From Counts to Conclusions is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 20 and 22 with Estefania Mancini (Josep Carreras Leukaemia Research Institute). Ecologists and environmental scientists often investigate complex biological systems where gene expression changes can reveal responses to environmental stressors, ecosystem health, or species interactions. Learning RNA-seq analysis in R/Bioconductor provides a powerful methodological framework to quantify these molecular responses, enabling researchers to link environmental factors to gene-level changes in their study organisms. The practical, statistically rigorous workflows taught will help you generate robust, publishable insights into ecological processes, such as how vegetation responds to climate change or how soil microbes react to pollutants.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/rna-seq-from-counts-to-conclusions) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: Machine Learning for Ecology & Biology in R
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: Machine Learning for Ecology & Biology in R
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - Discover how new computational methods can enhance your research and data analysis skills. Machine Learning for Computational Biology 3.0 is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 14 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Lund University). Machine learning is an indispensable tool for analyzing complex biological data, crucial for advancing research in various scientific fields. This workshop offers practical applications in R and Python, enabling participants to code algorithms like neural networks, random forests, k-means clustering, and MCMC from scratch. Ecologists and environmental scientists can apply these advanced methods to model complex ecological systems, analyze genomic data from environmental samples, or identify patterns in large environmental datasets using their R skills.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/machine-learning-for-computational-biolo-2) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: Deep Learning for Complex Datasets
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: Deep Learning for Complex Datasets
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - Explore how advanced deep learning techniques can be applied to your ecological and environmental data. Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 15 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Lund University). Integrating deep learning techniques can significantly advance research methodologies for analyzing complex ecological and environmental datasets. This seminar will equip researchers with practical skills to model intricate patterns and uncover hidden structures within their own vegetation, soil, or hydrology data using R, Python, and TensorFlow. Participants will learn to leverage AI 3.0 tools like ChatGPT to develop neural network architectures and refine models, directly enhancing their capacity to address complex scientific questions in environmental science.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/deep-learning-for-complex-datasets-with-4) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] online course -  Capture-Mark-Recapture Analyses in R
messages.subject_clean: online course -  Capture-Mark-Recapture Analyses in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
We are pleased to announce our upcoming online course Capture-Mark-Recapture Analyses in R, taking place from 6?8 October.
 
This hands-on training introduces the key concepts and models used in CMR analyses, including Cormack?Jolly?Seber and POPAN models. Participants will learn how to move from raw encounter data to model fitting, interpretation, and visualization using the RMark package in R.
 
The course is designed for researchers with basic experience in R who wish to apply CMR methods in their work, including those transitioning from MARK to R.
 
More information and registration: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/capture-mark-recapture/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/capture-mark-recapture/ ) 
 
We would be grateful if you could share this opportunity with interested colleagues.
 
Best regards,
 
 
 
Carlo

messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: Computational Genomics for Traits
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - Discover how cutting-edge computational approaches can enhance your analysis of complex ecological and environmental data. Computational Genomics for Microbial Traits (Free Seminar) is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 22 with Janani Ravi (University of Colorado Anschutz). This seminar introduces general-purpose computational approaches that integrate large-scale, heterogeneous public datasets to bridge the microbial genotype-phenotype gap. Researchers in ecology and environmental science can apply these methods, including machine learning and comparative genomics, to analyze microbial communities in diverse environments, linking genomic features to ecological traits or environmental outcomes. The presented open-source software and interactive web tools offer practical resources for investigating microbial adaptation, resistance, and host interactions relevant to ecological systems and environmental health.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/computational-genomics-for-microbial-tra) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Live online course: Web Scraping in R
messages.subject_clean: Live online course: Web Scraping in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

Transmitting Science is offering the live online course "Web Scraping in 
R".

Course webpage: 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/statistics-and-bioinformatics/web-scraping-in-r/

This course shows you how to use R's web scraping packages and functions 
to extract data from publicly available websites and online sources. 
You'll learn how to read and parse HTML, select relevant elements from a 
webpage, and convert the results into clean, analysis-ready tables in R.

Key Highlights:

  	* Web scraping with R packages: Use established R tools to download 
web pages, parse HTML, and extract text and tables.
  	* Selectors and page structure: Identify and target the right elements 
on a page (e.g., links, tables, headings, metadata).

	* End-to-end workflow in R: Collect, clean, transform, and export 
scraped data in a tidy format.

For any questions, please write to courses at transmittingscience.com

Best regards,

Haris

-- 
Haris Saslis, PhD
Course Coordinator
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com [1]



Links:
------
[1] http://www.transmittingscience.com

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (Online course) Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan
messages.subject_clean: (Online course) Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,
We are pleased to announce that a few last seats are still available for our upcoming online course:
Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan

Dates: 4?7 May 


Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ) 
 
This practical course introduces the main methods for analysing multivariate ecological data using R and the vegan package.
Topics include:
Unconstrained ordination (PCA, NMDS, PCoA)
Constrained ordination (RDA, CCA, db-RDA)
PERMANOVA and PERMDISP
Permutation-based statistical inference
Applications to ecological and high-throughput (metabarcoding/metagenomics) data
The course is hands-on, with guided exercises and practical examples throughout.
It is designed for PhD students and researchers with basic R experience who want to gain confidence in analysing multivariate data.
 
If you are interested in joining, we recommend registering soon as places are limited.
 
Best regards,
Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Single Cell Data Analysis
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Single Cell Data Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

This seminar offers an in-depth exploration of computational tools and methodologies for high-level data interpretation. Single Cell Data Analysis 2.0 led by Nikolay Oskolkov (Lund University) is now available as an on-demand streaming seminar. This seminar introduces advanced computational tools and methodologies for single-cell data interpretation, offering skills in preprocessing, dimensionality reduction, and clustering. Ecologists and environmental scientists can adapt these robust analytical techniques, particularly those using R and Python, to uncover heterogeneity and patterns within their complex ecological datasets. Mastering these data analysis pipelines will empower researchers to derive deeper insights and advance their understanding of intricate environmental systems.

Access this seminar (https://instats.org/seminar/single-cell-data-analysis-20-1) at your own pace and please share this with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] h3sdm: H3 hexagonal grids for polygon-based species distribution and landscape modeling in R
messages.subject_clean: h3sdm: H3 hexagonal grids for polygon-based species distribution and landscape modeling in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear list members,

I am pleased to announce that h3sdm is now available on CRAN.

h3sdm takes a different approach from traditional raster-based SDMs:
instead of working at the pixel level, it uses H3 hexagonal grids as the
unit of analysis. Environmental and land cover information is aggregated
within each hexagon, and information theory (IT) metrics are computed to
characterize spatial heterogeneity and landscape composition at the polygon
level.

This polygon-based framework makes the package suitable not only for
species distribution modeling, but also for habitat and landscape analysis
at regional or local scales ? watersheds, protected areas, or biological
corridors ? where pixel-level approaches are often too fine-grained or
noisy for ecological interpretation.

Modeling is built on tidymodels, allowing users to fit any classification
or regression algorithm available in that ecosystem ? from GAMs to random
forests or gradient boosting ? within the same reproducible workflow.

Key features:
- H3 hexagonal grid integration
- Extraction of continuous and categorical environmental variables per
hexagon
- Calculation of information theory metrics to characterize within-polygon
heterogeneity
- tidymodels-based modeling pipeline, open to any supported algorithm

Install from CRAN:

  install.packages("h3sdm")

GitHub: https://github.com/ManuelSpinola/h3sdm

Feedback and contributions are welcome.

Best regards,

Manuel Spinola

-- 
*Manuel Sp?nola, Ph.D.*
Instituto Internacional en Conservaci?n y Manejo de Vida Silvestre
Universidad Nacional
Apartado 1350-3000
Heredia
COSTA RICA
mspinola at una.cr <mspinola at una.ac.cr>
mspinola10 at gmail.com
Tel?fono: (506) 8706 - 4662
Sitio web institucional: ICOMVIS
<http://www.icomvis.una.ac.cr/index.php/manuel>
Sitio web personal: Sitio personal <https://mspinola-sitioweb.netlify.app>
Blog sobre Ciencia de Datos: Blog de Ciencia de Datos
<https://mspinola-ciencia-de-datos.netlify.app>

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Bayesian modelling and evolutionary analysis using RevBayes - online, 21-24 April
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian modelling and evolutionary analysis using RevBayes - online, 21-24 April
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We have just a few seats left for our upcoming online course:
A hands-on course in Bayesian modelling and evolutionary analysis using RevBayes

Dates: 21?24 April 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/bayesian-phylogenetics/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/bayesian-phylogenetics/ )
 
 
 
This course offers an in-depth introduction to Bayesian inference and MCMC methods for phylogenetic analysis, with a strong focus on hands-on learning using RevBayes. Across four interactive days, participants will learn how to build and interpret models of lineage diversification, biogeographic history, and evolutionary processes using real datasets.
 
 
Topics include:
Foundations of Bayesian inference and probabilistic graphical models
Biogeographic modelling (including DEC and stochastic mapping)
Comparative biogeography (Bayesian Island Biogeography)
Diversification analyses within birth?death frameworks
 
 
 
Feel free to reach out if you have any questions.
 
Best regards,
Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D
